metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Split the last AC into two separate criteria, each with a named pytest function: one for configurability (e.g., `::test_config_knobs`) and one for error handling (e.g., `::test_error_yields_empty`).
findings.finding: The last acceptance criterion bundles two distinct observable outcomes — (1) knobs are configurable and (2) a graph-read/scoring error yields an empty list — into a single criterion with no named test function for the error-handling assertion, unlike all other ACs which name specific pytest functions.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — last bullet: 'Field weights, max_doc_freq, overlap floor, and K are configurable via the overlap typed config; a graph-read/scoring error yields an empty list (never blocks the caller).'
findings.suggested_fix:
Replace the last AC bullet with:
- [ ] Field weights, `max_doc_freq`, overlap floor, and K are overridable via `LLMConfig` / env vars and produce different ranking results; verified by `::test_config_knobs`.
- [ ] A graph-read or scoring exception is caught and returns `[]` without propagating; verified by `::test_error_yields_empty`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how `related_ticket_ids` obtains the `tracker` filesystem path required by `list_descendants(root_id, tracker)` — e.g., accept it as a parameter (`related_ticket_ids(ticket_id, tracker)`) or document the config/context resolver it calls.
findings.finding: `list_descendants(root_id: str, tracker: str) -> dict` requires a `tracker` filesystem-path argument, but the proposed helper signature `related_ticket_ids(ticket_id) -> set[str]` takes only `ticket_id`. The plan never states how the tracker path is obtained inside the new helper, making the reuse claim unimplementable as written.
findings.suggested_fix: Change the helper signature to `related_ticket_ids(ticket_id: str, tracker: str) -> set[str]` (threading `tracker` from the caller, consistent with how every other `_engine_support` module accepts it), and update the interface contract for `retrieve(query_digest, corpus, exclude)` to clarify where `tracker` is provided.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Amend the `related_ticket_ids` signature (or its description) to specify how `tracker` is obtained — e.g. `related_ticket_ids(ticket_id, tracker: str) -> set[str]` or document that it is resolved internally via `rebar.config.tracker_dir()`.
findings.finding: The proposed helper signature `related_ticket_ids(ticket_id) -> set[str]` omits the `tracker` parameter that `list_descendants(root_id, tracker)` requires (confirmed at `descendants.py:38`). The plan gives no account of how `graph.py` obtains the tracker filesystem path.
findings.location: What section — 'A NEW helper `src/rebar/llm/overlap/graph.py::related_ticket_ids(ticket_id) -> set[str]` that REUSES the existing public `src/rebar/_engine_support/descendants.py::list_descendants`'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how the `tracker` path (needed by `list_descendants`) is resolved and threaded into `related_ticket_ids` — e.g. add a `tracker` parameter to `related_ticket_ids` (and expose it via `retrieve`'s call site), or resolve it via `current_tickets_root()` / `LLMConfig.tickets_path` inside `graph.py`, matching the pattern already used by the rest of the LLM gate machinery.
findings.finding: The plan says `related_ticket_ids` will REUSE `list_descendants(root_id, tracker)`, but `list_descendants` requires a filesystem `tracker` directory path — not a digest-corpus object — and `retrieve.py` only holds `ticket_digest` records. The plan provides no mechanism for threading the `tracker` path into `graph.py::related_ticket_ids`, leaving its calling convention undefined and the reuse contract unresolvable by the implementer.
findings.location: Scope section: 'new `src/rebar/llm/overlap/graph.py` (`related_ticket_ids`, reusing `descendants.list_descendants`)'; also Mechanism section same wording
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `tracker: str` parameter to `related_ticket_ids(ticket_id, tracker)` and resolve it at the `retrieve.py` call site from `cfg.tickets_path` (or `current_tickets_root()` with a checkout fallback), consistent with how other LLM gate ops resolve the ticket-store root.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `related_ticket_ids` must collect linked tickets from BOTH the query ticket's own `deps` (outbound) AND by scanning the corpus for any ticket whose `deps` reference the query ticket (inbound), or alternatively call `build_dep_graph` (which already aggregates children, blockers, and deps bidirectionally) to capture the full linked set.
findings.finding: The graph-exclusion filter reads 'linked tickets (from reduced `deps`)' — but `deps` in the rebar data model are DIRECTIONAL. Only `relates_to` writes a reciprocal LINK event; `blocks`, `depends_on`, `duplicates`, `supersedes`, and `discovered_from` are one-directional. Reading only the query ticket's own `deps` misses all inbound links (e.g. if ticket B `depends_on` or `relates_to`-from the query ticket A but the reciprocal entry was not written or was compacted). The exclusion set will be silently incomplete for those relation types.
findings.location: What section: 'Graph-exclusion filter … linked tickets (from reduced `deps`)'; `graph.py::related_ticket_ids` description
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the 'from reduced `deps`' description with: call `build_dep_graph(ticket_id, tracker)` which returns `{deps, blockers, children}` — `deps` covers outbound links, `blockers` covers inbound blocking links, and add a corpus-scan for inbound non-blocking relations (`relates_to`, `duplicates`, `supersedes`, `discovered_from`) from tickets whose `deps[].target_id == query_ticket_id`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the `related_ticket_ids` signature with `list_descendants`'s required `tracker: str` argument — either add `tracker` (or a `LLMConfig`/config-derived path) as an explicit parameter, or document exactly how `graph.py` resolves the store path without it.
findings.finding: `list_descendants(root_id, tracker)` requires a `tracker` filesystem-path argument, but the proposed `related_ticket_ids(ticket_id) -> set[str]` signature exposes only `ticket_id`. The plan provides no mechanism for the helper to obtain the `tracker` path, making the stated reuse of `list_descendants` unimplementable as described — the caller cannot invoke it without a store path.
findings.location: What section — 'A NEW helper `src/rebar/llm/overlap/graph.py::related_ticket_ids(ticket_id) -> set[str]` that REUSES the existing public `src/rebar/_engine_support/descendants.py::list_descendants` for descendants'
findings.suggested_fix: Change the proposed signature to `related_ticket_ids(ticket_id: str, tracker: str) -> set[str]` (threading the store path explicitly), matching the pattern used by every other caller of `list_descendants` in the codebase, and update the AC/test fixture accordingly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge and bound the `reduce_all_tickets` I/O cost introduced by reusing `list_descendants`, either by scoping the perf AC to cover the full `retrieve()` call (graph exclusion + scoring), or by specifying that `related_ticket_ids` accepts a pre-loaded `all_states` dict to avoid a second full-scan when the caller already holds one.
findings.finding: `list_descendants` internally calls `reduce_all_tickets(tracker)` — a full O(n) disk scan over the entire ticket store. Invoking it as part of the per-query graph-exclusion filter (which runs BEFORE BM25F scoring for every overlap check) incurs this disk-scan cost on every call. The plan asserts '<10ms at ~300 digests' for the scoring step but does not budget or acknowledge the `reduce_all_tickets` I/O cost introduced by the `list_descendants` reuse.
findings.location: What section — 'REUSES the existing public `src/rebar/_engine_support/descendants.py::list_descendants` for descendants'; AC — '<10ms at ~300 digests; verified by `pytest tests/unit/test_overlap_retrieve.py::{test_topk,test_perf}`'

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `_SECTIONS['verify']['overlap_enabled']` (with a `_as_bool` coercer) to the Scope's edit-set for `src/rebar/_config_schema.py`.
findings.finding: The plan adds `overlap_enabled` to `VerifyConfig` (in `src/rebar/_config_schema.py`) but does not enumerate the required parallel edit to the `_SECTIONS['verify']` coercer dict (lines 422–438), which must include a `'overlap_enabled': lambda v, k: _as_bool(v, k)` entry for the key to be recognized rather than silently warned-and-dropped at load time.
findings.location: Scope section — 'new `overlap_*` keys on `LLMConfig` … `overlap_enabled` lives in `VerifyConfig` `[verify]`'
findings.suggested_fix: In `_SECTIONS['verify']`, add: `"overlap_enabled": lambda v, k: _as_bool(v, k),`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the interface signature with the exclusion design: either remove the `exclude` parameter and document that `retrieve` calls `related_ticket_ids` internally, OR keep `exclude` as caller-supplied and update the What section to state that the caller (S7) is responsible for constructing the exclusion set using `related_ticket_ids`.
findings.finding: The What section describes graph-exclusion as an internally-derived operation (using `related_ticket_ids(ticket_id)` called inside the function), but the Scope section's canonical interface signature is `retrieve(query_digest, corpus, exclude)` — with an explicit caller-supplied `exclude` parameter. These two descriptions contradict each other: one implies the function owns the exclusion logic, the other implies the caller computes and passes it. An implementer reading both sections would not know which design to build.
findings.location: What section (graph-exclusion paragraph) vs. Scope section (interface contract `retrieve(query_digest, corpus, exclude)`)
findings.suggested_fix: Remove `exclude` from the public interface signature and document that `retrieve(query_digest, corpus)` calls `related_ticket_ids(query_digest.ticket_id)` internally before scoring. This keeps graph-exclusion encapsulated and consistent with the What section's description. Update the interface contract to: `retrieve(query_digest, corpus) -> list[OverlapCandidate]`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the default numeric values for field weights (e.g., `problem_keywords=2.0, component_or_area=2.0, propositions=1.0, key_entities=1.0`), `max_doc_freq` (e.g., `0.6` = appears in >60% of corpus), and the overlap floor (e.g., `1` matched term minimum) in the plan or config schema.
findings.finding: Default numeric values for field weights (problem_keywords, component_or_area, propositions, key_entities), `max_doc_freq`, and the `minimum_should_match` overlap floor are not stated anywhere in the plan, leaving the executor to invent them without guidance.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a defaults table to the Scope section, e.g.: `overlap_k=20, overlap_weight_problem_keywords=2.0, overlap_weight_component_or_area=2.0, overlap_weight_propositions=1.0, overlap_weight_key_entities=1.0, overlap_max_doc_freq=0.6, overlap_min_match=1`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the default value for `overlap_enabled` in the plan (e.g., `overlap_enabled = false` so the feature is opt-in until S7 wires it).
findings.finding: The `overlap_enabled` gate flag in `VerifyConfig` has no stated default (enabled or disabled), so an executor implementing the flag cannot know whether the feature is on or off out of the box.
findings.location: Scope section — 'Only the gate ENABLE flag overlap_enabled lives in VerifyConfig [verify]'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `overlap_enabled = false  # opt-in; S7 flips this when wiring review_plan` to the Scope section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge that `list_descendants` returns a bucketed dict and specify which buckets to union into the exclusion set (e.g., `epics ∪ stories ∪ tasks ∪ bugs`) for `related_ticket_ids`.
findings.finding: `list_descendants` returns a bucketed dict `{epics, stories, tasks, bugs, parents_with_children}`, not a flat set of descendant IDs. The plan implies it directly provides an excludable ID set, but the helper must additionally flatten the buckets — an unacknowledged transformation step.
findings.location: What section — 'REUSES the existing public `src/rebar/_engine_support/descendants.py::list_descendants` for descendants'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the concrete type for each parameter of `retrieve` in the interface contract (e.g. `retrieve(query_digest: TicketDigest, corpus: list[TicketDigest], exclude: set[str]) -> list[OverlapCandidate]`).
findings.finding: The `corpus` parameter in the stated interface `retrieve(query_digest, corpus, exclude) -> list[OverlapCandidate]` has no type annotation in the plan — an executor cannot write the function signature or callers without knowing whether `corpus` is a `list[TicketDigest]`, a `dict[str, TicketDigest]`, or something else.
findings.location: Scope section — interface contract: `retrieve(query_digest, corpus, exclude) -> list[OverlapCandidate]`
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Scope: `retrieve(query_digest: TicketDigest, corpus: list[TicketDigest], exclude: set[str]) -> list[OverlapCandidate]` — `TicketDigest` is the S1-defined dataclass; `exclude` is a pre-computed set of ticket_id strings (the caller may pass `related_ticket_ids(...)` directly).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Move the BM25F tuning knobs (`field_weights`, `max_doc_freq`, `stopword_list`, `overlap_floor`, `K`) out of `LLMConfig` / `[tool.rebar.llm]` and into either a lightweight `OverlapConfig` dataclass in `retrieve.py` (with constants as defaults) or inline them as named module-level constants — only promoting to a config-file key if operator-facing tunability is demonstrated to be needed at this stage.
findings.finding: Five or more BM25F tuning knobs (`field weights`, `max_doc_freq`, `stopword list`, `minimum_should_match` floor, `K`) are routed into `LLMConfig` under `REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_*` env vars — the config layer that owns model IDs, token budgets, and per-call timeouts. BM25F is a deterministic, no-LLM scorer explicitly described as 'no LLM and no embeddings'. Placing non-LLM algorithmic knobs in `LLMConfig` conflates two orthogonal concerns and proliferates the `LLMConfig` surface with keys that have no relationship to the LLM runtime.
findings.location: Scope section — 'overlap config weights/knobs in the `rebar.llm` config layer: new `overlap_*` keys on `LLMConfig` (`src/rebar/llm/config.py`, `[tool.rebar.llm]`, env `REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_*`, registered in `LLMConfig.from_env`)'
findings.suggested_fix: Define a simple `@dataclass OverlapConfig` in `retrieve.py` with field defaults (e.g. `k: int = 20`, `max_doc_freq: float = 0.5`) instantiated directly by the caller or by a constructor that reads the few keys from `LLMConfig` only if they already belong there. Reserve `REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_*` env-var proliferation for a follow-up stage once the defaults are validated on real data.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/llm/overlap/__init__.py` to the Scope's edit-set so the new `overlap` directory is a proper importable Python package.
findings.finding: The Scope section lists two new files (`retrieve.py`, `graph.py`) in the new `src/rebar/llm/overlap/` package directory but never mentions `__init__.py`. Without an `__init__.py`, the directory is not a regular Python package and `from rebar.llm.overlap.retrieve import ...` will fail unless the project is configured for implicit namespace packages.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `src/rebar/llm/overlap/__init__.py` (empty or with a brief module docstring) to the Scope's list of new files.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief ADR or CLAUDE.md entry capturing (a) why a bespoke BM25F scorer was chosen over rank-bm25 and (b) the deliberate config split between LLMConfig (tunables) and VerifyConfig (gate flag).
findings.finding: No ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design-doc entry is planned for two novel architectural decisions introduced here: (1) a bespoke BM25F field-weighting implementation (rationale: rank-bm25 is unweighted) and (2) the deliberate two-layer config split (overlap knobs in LLMConfig, gate flag in VerifyConfig). Future maintainers encountering this split will have no recorded rationale.
findings.location: Scope section / 'overlap config weights/knobs in the rebar.llm config layer'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `docs/adr/overlap-bm25f-scorer.md` (or an entry in CLAUDE.md) with: problem statement, options considered (rank-bm25 vs bespoke), decision, and the config-layer split rationale. This is ~10 lines and prevents the intent from being lost.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Instrument the retrieval path with at least two counters (e.g., `overlap.skipped_digests` and `overlap.retrieval_errors`) so operators can monitor corpus health and error rates without log mining.
findings.finding: Observability for the new retrieval path is limited to ad-hoc log statements (skip-count log, exception log). There are no structured counters or metrics for: (a) retrieval error rate, (b) corpus skip rate (absent/stale digests as a fraction of corpus size), or (c) per-call latency. Operators cannot distinguish a slow-degrading corpus (steadily rising skip rate) from a healthy run without reading raw logs.
findings.location: What section / 'Count skipped tickets and log the count' / 'a graph-read or scoring exception is caught, logged'
findings.suggested_fix: In `retrieve.py`, increment a simple in-process counter or emit a structured log entry with `skip_count`, `corpus_size`, and `error` fields at a consistent log level (e.g., WARNING when skip_rate > 20%). If the project already uses a metrics library (e.g., Prometheus, statsd), emit gauges there; otherwise structured logging with machine-parseable keys is sufficient.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm at least one additional named call-site for `related_ticket_ids` (e.g., name the S7 integration point explicitly), or justify the standalone `graph.py` module by stating it is the designated seam S7 will import — if there is only one call-site now, consider inlining the exclusion logic into `retrieve.py` and extracting `graph.py` when S7 actually needs it.
findings.finding: `related_ticket_ids` is proposed as a standalone exported helper in its own module (`graph.py`) but has exactly one confirmed call-site in this stage (the exclusion filter in `retrieve.py`). Its second potential consumer (S7 / review-plan wiring) is explicitly OUT of scope for this stage, so the Rule-of-Three threshold (≥3 call-sites) is not met. The abstraction may be justified by anticipated reuse, but the plan does not affirmatively enumerate those future consumers — it only says 'S7 isn't blind to it' for the OverlapCandidate interface, not for `related_ticket_ids`.
findings.location: What section — 'A NEW helper `src/rebar/llm/overlap/graph.py::related_ticket_ids(ticket_id) -> set[str]`'; Scope section — 'OUT: the LLM judge (S6), review-plan wiring (S7)'
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) inline `related_ticket_ids` into `retrieve.py` as a private helper until S7 confirms it reuses the function, or (b) explicitly name the S7 call-site in the Scope section so the Rule-of-Three evidence is in the plan text.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify whether any `overlap_*` config keys are boolean and, if so, enumerate adding `_llm_bool` (or an equivalent resolver) to `src/rebar/llm/config.py` in the Scope.
findings.finding: The plan routes `overlap_*` weight/knob keys through `LLMConfig` via `REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_*` env vars and `LLMConfig.from_env`, but `LLMConfig.from_env` (lines 458–524) has no boolean resolver (`_llm_bool` does not exist in `config.py`). Any boolean overlap flag (e.g. a stopword-enabled toggle) cannot be wired without first adding this helper, which is an unenumerated edit target within the already-named `config.py`.
findings.location: Scope section — 'new `overlap_*` keys on `LLMConfig` … registered in `LLMConfig.from_env`'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
